results.seo_title: Implementing SHAP Explainability Workflows for Machine Learning…
results.meta_description: Learn how to implement SHAP explainability workflows with Explainer Comparisons, Maskers, Interactions, Drift, and Black-Box Models for machine learning model…
results.body_html:
What is SHAP Explainability Workflow? A practical framework for interpreting machine learning models beyond basic feature-importance plots.

Training Tree-Based ModelsStart by training tree-based models using popular libraries such as Scikit-learn or TensorFlow.Use techniques like bagging and boosting to improve model accuracy and reduce overfitting.Comparing SHAP ExplainersMethodAccuracyRuntimeTree MethodHigh accuracy, but slow runtimeSlow runtime, but high accuracyExact MethodModerate accuracy, fast runtimeFast runtime, moderate accuracyPermutation MethodLow accuracy, fast runtimeFast runtime, low accuracyKernel MethodHigh accuracy, fast runtimeFast runtime, high accuracyBlack-Box Models and Drift DetectionUse Black-Box Models to detect drift in data distributions and improve model performance.

source.title: A Coding Guide Implementing SHAP Explainability Workflows with Explainer Comparisons, Maskers, Interactions, Drift, and Black-Box Models
source.summary:
In this tutorial, we implement SHAP workflows as a practical framework for interpreting machine learning models beyond basic feature-importance plots. We start by training tree-based models and then compare different SHAP explainers, including Tree, Exact, Permutation, and Kernel methods, to understand how accuracy and runtime change across model-aware and model-agnostic approaches. We also examine how […]

The post A Coding Guide Implementing SHAP Explainability Workflows with Explainer Comparisons, Maskers, Interactions, Drift, and Black-Box Models appeared first on MarkTechPost.

results.seo_title: Chatbots at the Drive-Thru: How AI is Revolutionizing Fast Food
results.meta_description: Discover how chatbots are transforming the fast food industry with AI-powered drive-thrus, improving efficiency and customer experience.
results.body_html:
Chatbots at the drive-thru are just the beginning, as fast food chains adopt AI-powered technology to improve efficiency and customer experience.

Mcdonald's deployed AI chatbots at 10 locations in Chicago in 2021, marking one of the first major fast-food chains to do so. Checkers and Rally’s teamed up with Presto to put a chatbot at all corporate-owned drive-thrus in the US in 2022. Taco Bell launched its “FreshAI” chatbot at one of its drive-thrus in Columbus, Ohio in 2023.

BenefitDescription Improved Order AccuracyAchieved with 86% of orders correct without employee intervention at Wendy’s. Reduced Employee Task LoadPitch by Taco Bell to slash drive-thru wait times and reduce labor costs. Increased EfficiencyEnabled by automation, freeing up staff for more people-dependent areas of their business.

Future Plans and Expansion Taco Bell plans to expand its Voice AI drive-thru technology to hundreds of locations in the US by the end of 2024.

source.title: Chatbots at the drive-thru are just the beginning
source.summary: This is The Stepback, a weekly newsletter breaking down one essential story from the tech world. For more news about how AI is seeping into our daily lives, follow Emma Roth. The Stepback arrives in our subscribers' inboxes at 8AM ET. Opt in for The Stepback here. How it started In 2021, McDonald's became one […]

results.seo_title: Geometry-Aware Contrastive Learning for Few-Shot Automatic…
results.meta_description: A new framework for automatic modulation recognition that achieves a 6.27% accuracy gain in 1-shot settings.
results.body_html:
DyCo-CL achieves a 6.27% accuracy gain in 1-shot settings over prior methods.

Key Components of DyCo-CLVirtual Adversarial Augmentation (VAA) with semantic consistency lossSignal-Adaptive Swin Backbone with fixed-window attentionHybrid Knowledge Fusion module for anchoring representations with physical priorsTheoretical Analysis of DyCo-CLDyCo-CL acts as an implicit spectral regularizer for the encoder, enabling stable manifold exploration.Experimental Results and BenchmarkingRML BenchmarkAccuracy Gain1-shot settings6.27%

source.title: Geometry-Aware Contrastive Learning for Few-Shot Automatic Modulation Recognition
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new framework called Dynamic-Consistency Contrastive Learning (DyCo-CL) to improve Automatic Modulation Recognition (AMR). The approach addresses challenges such as ineffective augmentations and spectral instability by combining Virtual Adversarial Augmentation with a semantic consistency loss. Experiments on RML benchmarks show that DyCo-CL achieves significant accuracy gains, particularly in 1-shot settings.

results.seo_title: Respecting Modality Gap in Post-hoc Out-of-distribution Detection…
results.meta_description: Researchers challenge the text-as-prototype paradigm and present an online pseudo-supervised framework to mitigate the modality gap in post-hoc OOD detection.
results.body_html:
The modality gap in post-hoc out-of-distribution detection with pre-trained vision-language models refers to the misalignment between textual and visual prototypes. This gap cannot be eliminated by prompt engineering alone, according to recent research.

Challenges of Text-as-Prototype Paradigm

Existing methods treat text embeddings of class names as class prototypes, but these prototypes are generally misaligned with the optimal visual prototypes. The modality gap yields an intrinsic limitation that cannot be overcome by prompt engineering alone. Text-as-prototype paradigm is widely adopted, but its limitations have not been thoroughly explored.

Proposed Online Pseudo-Supervised Framework

Visual Feature Space Soft Predictions from Pre-trained VLMs

Directly learns class prototypes in the visual feature space using unlabeled test-time data streams. Soft predictions from pre-trained VLMs are used to guide the online optimization procedure.

Theoretical Guarantees for Convergence Our method achieves theoretical guarantees for the convergence of the online optimization procedure, ensuring that the modality gap is effectively mitigated.

Empirical Results and State-of-the-Art Performance

Our method achieves a new state of the art across a variety of OOD detection setups.

source.title: Respecting Modality Gap in Post-hoc Out-of-distribution Detection with Pre-trained Vision-Language Models
source.summary: Researchers have challenged the common practice of using text embeddings as class prototypes in out-of-distribution (OOD) detection with pre-trained vision-language models, finding that these textual prototypes are often misaligned with optimal visual prototypes due to an intrinsic modality gap. To address this issue, a new online pseudo-supervised framework is proposed that learns class prototypes directly from unlabeled test-time data streams and soft predictions from the pre-trained model. The method achieves state-of-the-art performance across various OOD detection setups.

results.seo_title: DynFrame: Adaptive Reasoning-Driven Multimodal Framework for Complex…
results.meta_description: DynFrame is a framework that addresses structural gaps in existing thinking-with-video systems by emitting temporal window and sampling density as native…
results.body_html:
DynFrame is a framework that addresses structural gaps in existing thinking-with-video systems by emitting temporal window and sampling density as native tokens within a single autoregressive pass.

Emits temporal window and sampling density as native tokens within a single autoregressive pass Enables acquiring multi-granularity evidence with a single retrieval step Splits each rollout at the retrieval boundary and assigns role-specific token-level advantages Trained on curated DM-CoT-74k and DM-RL-45k datasets, achieving competitive performance with strong baselines

Advantages of DynFrame over Existing Methods

Sampling Density DynFrame's learnable span-density retrieval enables acquiring multi-granularity evidence with a single retrieval step

Temporal Window DynFrame emits temporal window as native tokens, allowing for more efficient and effective video understanding

Benchmarks and Results Trained on the curated DM-CoT-74k and DM-RL-45k datasets, DynFrame-4B is competitive with strong 7B-8B baselines across six benchmarks (NExT-GQA, Charades-STA, ActivityNet-MR, Video-MME, MLVU, LVBench), and DynFrame-8B sets new state-of-the-art on most metrics.

source.title: DynFrame: Adaptive Reasoning-Driven Multimodal Framework with Dynamic Frame Augmentation for Complex Video Understanding
source.summary: Researchers have introduced a new framework called DynFrame, which aims to improve the performance of video understanding models by addressing two structural gaps in existing thinking-with-video systems. The framework uses a learnable span-density retrieval mechanism that enables acquiring multi-granularity evidence with a single retrieval step. DynFrame has been trained on large datasets and achieved competitive results with strong baselines across six benchmarks, setting a new state-of-the-art on most metrics.

results.seo_title: Cross-Lingual Generalization in Language Models: What Works and Why
results.meta_description: An in-vitro study on cross-lingual generalization in language models reveals that tokenization preserves reusable cross-lingual substructure is key to…
results.body_html:
Transfer in language models is governed less by tokenizer balance or raw lexical similarity than by whether tokenization preserves reusable cross-lingual substructure.

Smaller vocabularies often improve masked transfer by keeping words decomposable into shared fragments. Larger vocabularies can turn forms into language-specific atoms. Tokenization preserves reusable cross-lingual substructure is key to successful transfer.

Grammatical and type-level competence Precede masked lexical generalization.

Masked lexical generalization FOLLOWs grammatical and type-level competence.

Bridge Strength Correlates with Masked Reachability Bridge strength correlates strongly with masked reachability, suggesting a key mechanism underlying transfer in language models.

source.title: An In-Vitro Study on Cross-Lingual Generalization in Language Models
source.summary: Researchers have developed an in-vitro framework to study cross-lingual generalization in language models. The study uses two procedurally generated languages with the same underlying structure but different surface realizations, allowing for controlled experiments on factors affecting transfer. The results show that tokenization plays a crucial role in determining whether transfer occurs, and smaller vocabularies tend to improve masked transfer by breaking down words into reusable fragments.

results.seo_title: Stabilizing Recurrent Dynamics for Test-Time Scalable Latent…
results.meta_description: A new training framework, STARS, is proposed to stabilize recurrent dynamics and improve test-time scaling in looped language models.
results.body_html:
Looped Language Models (LoopLMs) enable efficient latent reasoning through depth recurrence, yet exhibit unreliable test-time scaling behavior.

Current Limitations of LoopLMs

  • Performance often peaks at a certain iteration depth and then collapses with further recurrence.
  • Existing architectures and strategies exhibit an inherent trade-off between stability and effectiveness.

Proposed Solution: STARS (STAbility-driven Recurrent Scaling)

Converges toward stable fixed points while preserving effectiveness represents a promising way to stabilize recurrent dynamics.

STARS constrains latent states to approach asymptotically stable fixed points via efficient Jacobian Spectral Radius Regularization with random loop sampling.

Experimental Results

TaskPerformance on Test-Time Scaling
Arithmetic TasksReliable test-time scaling achieved by STARS.
Complex Mathematical ReasoningSubstantial mitigation of performance degradation as recurrence depth increases, while improving peak performance.

source.title: Stabilizing Recurrent Dynamics for Test-Time Scalable Latent Reasoning in Looped Language Models
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new training framework called STARS (STAbility-driven Recurrent Scaling) to improve the scalability of Looped Language Models. The framework constrains latent states to approach asymptotically stable fixed points, enabling reliable test-time scaling and mitigating performance degradation as recurrence depth increases. This approach is based on uncertainty reduction and uses efficient Jacobian Spectral Radius Regularization with random loop sampling. Experiments have shown promising results in both arithmetic tasks and complex mathematical reasoning.

results.seo_title: Towards Generalization-Oriented Models for Vehicle Routing Problems…
results.meta_description: A new approach to improve cross-distribution generalization in vehicle routing problems using Residual Refined Experts with Instance-level Gating.
results.body_html:
What is the optimal set of routes for a fleet of vehicles to traverse in order to deliver to a given set of customers?

Improving Cross-Distribution Generalization

Residual Refined Experts with Instance-level Gating (R2E-IG) improves cross-distribution generalization by partitioning the policy network into multiple modules and adaptively recombining modules to form specific policies during inference. The proposed architecture enhances expert expressiveness via residual refinement, learns distribution-aware instance representations, and dynamically reweights training data from different distributions to emphasize more informative ones.

Introduces a Residual Refined Expert (R2E) architecture that enhances expert expressiveness via residual refinement. Designs an instance-level gating mechanism that learns distribution-aware instance representations and routes inputs to suitable modules. Proposes a mixed-distribution training mechanism equipped with Dynamic Weight Adaption (DWA), which dynamically reweights training data from different distributions to emphasize more informative ones.

Performance on Synthetic and Benchmark Datasets

R2E-IG Competitive performance against state-of-the-art baselines on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution instances.

DWA Dynamically reweights training data from different distributions to emphasize more informative ones, improving generalization.

source.title: Towards Generalization-Oriented Models for Vehicle Routing Problems with Mixture-of-Experts
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new approach to improve the performance of Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) models on Vehicle Routing Problems (VRPs), which are commonly used in logistics and transportation. The proposed model, called Residual Refined Experts with Instance-level Gating (R2E-IG), uses a modular architecture that adapts to different distributions during inference. This approach enables the model to generalize better across different scenarios and datasets. Experimental results show that R2E-IG outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution instances.

results.seo_title: Periodic Topological Deep Learning for Polymer Design and Discovery
results.meta_description: A deep learning framework captures many-body interactions across multiple spatial scales to predict polymer properties.
results.body_html:
Periodic Topological Deep Learning (Periodic-TDL) is a deep learning framework that captures many-body interactions across multiple spatial scales to predict polymer properties.

Represents polymers as molecular graphs with periodicity and many-body interactions Captures higher-order topological features through hierarchical simplicial message-passing (HSMP) encoder Outperforms state-of-the-art models across polymer property prediction tasks

Predicted Trends in Polymer Properties

Functional Group ModificationPredicted Thermal Stability Increase (°C)

Validation of Periodic-TDL Model Experimental data confirmed the model's predictions for six novel polymer pairs, including three newly synthesized polymers previously unreported in the literature.

Captures underlying physical effects of specific functional group modifications Outperforms state-of-the-art models across polymer property prediction tasks

source.title: Periodic Topological Deep Learning for Polymer Design and Discovery
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new deep learning framework called Periodic-TDL to improve the discovery and design of polymers for various applications. The framework can capture many-body interactions across multiple spatial scales and outperforms existing models in predicting polymer properties. By analyzing data from systematically generated structures, the researchers found that specific functional group modifications can significantly enhance thermal stability. Experimental verification confirmed the model's predictions, demonstrating its ability to accurately capture underlying physical effects.

results.seo_title: Eroding Trust in Real Speech: A Large-Scale Study of Human Audio…
results.meta_description: A recent study reveals that humans are increasingly distrusting authentic speech, shifting skepticism from fake samples to real ones.
results.body_html:
Human accuracy on fake samples barely changed, but accuracy on real samples dropped from 72.7% to 64.1%, according to the largest listening study on audio deepfake perception to date.

Participants increasingly distrust authentic speech, shifting skepticism from fake samples to real ones. Commercial and autoregressive language model systems proved hardest to detect (61.3 - 65.9%), while traditional seq2seq and flow-matching models remain easier to spot (75.4 - 76.8%). An ML detector maintained over 94.5% accuracy across all conditions.

System TypeAverage Accuracy on Fake SamplesAverage Accuracy on Real Samples

Commercial and Autoregressive Language Model Systems61.3 - 65.9%64.1% Traditional Seq2seq and Flow-Matching Models75.4 - 76.8%72.7%

Conclusion The primary threat posed by modern deepfakes may not be mere deception, but the erosion of trust in genuine audio.

source.title: Eroding Trust in Real Speech: A Large-Scale Study of Human Audio Deepfake Perception
source.summary: Researchers conducted a large-scale study on human perception of audio deepfakes, collecting data from 1,768 participants and 138 text-to-speech systems. The study found that while people's accuracy at detecting synthesized speech samples remained relatively stable, their ability to detect authentic speech decreased significantly. This suggests that the primary threat posed by modern deepfakes may not be convincing deception, but rather eroding trust in genuine audio.

results.seo_title: Compress and Benchmark LLMs with FP8, GPTQ, and SmoothQuant…
results.meta_description: Learn how to compress and benchmark instruction-tuned LLMs with FP8, GPTQ, and SmoothQuant quantization using llmcompressor.
results.body_html:
Can you compress and benchmark LLMs with FP8, GPTQ, and SmoothQuant quantization using llmcompressor? Yes, with this tutorial.

Step 1: Install llmcompressor

  • Download the llmcompressor repository from GitHub.
  • Clone the repository using git clone https://github.com/MarkTechPost/llmcompressor.git.
  • Change into the cloned directory and run pip install -r requirements.txt.

Step 2: Prepare your LLM model

Prepare your instruction-tuned language model by downloading the pre-trained weights.

Model NamePre-trained Weights
FP16 BaselineYour FP16 baseline model
GPTQ W4A16Your GPTQ W4A16 model
SmoothQuant with GPTQ W8A8Your SmoothQuant with GPTQ W8A8 model

Step 3: Compress and benchmark your LLM model

Use llmcompressor compress --model your-llm-model to compress your LLM model.

source.title: A Coding Implementation to Compress and Benchmark Instruction-Tuned LLMs with FP8, GPTQ, and SmoothQuant Quantization using llmcompressor
source.summary:
In this tutorial, we explore how to apply post-training quantization to an instruction-tuned language model using llmcompressor. We start with an FP16 baseline and then compare multiple compression strategies, including FP8 dynamic quantization, GPTQ W4A16, and SmoothQuant with GPTQ W8A8. Along the way, we benchmark each model variant for disk size, generation latency, throughput, perplexity, […]

The post A Coding Implementation to Compress and Benchmark Instruction-Tuned LLMs with FP8, GPTQ, and SmoothQuant Quantization using llmcompressor appeared first on MarkTechPost.

results.seo_title: The AI Fight Brewing Inside The New York Times
results.meta_description: The New York Times employees are gearing up for a fight over the use of AI in the newsroom, with concerns about job security and performance metrics.
results.body_html:
The New York Times employees are gearing up for a fight over the use of AI in the newsroom, with concerns about job security and performance metrics.

How AI is being used at The New York Times

  • DX, an internal AI tool, tracks employee output, generative AI use, and efficiency, but its personalized benchmarks have raised concerns about job security.
  • The union has filed grievances saying the company violated their collective bargaining agreement when it started using DX.

What are the implications of AI on journalism?

ImplicationsDescription
Job securityThe use of AI in the newsroom has raised concerns about job security, particularly for those working in data analysis and other roles.
Benchmarks and metricsThe personalized benchmarks from DX have been criticized for flattening all work into an opaque set of metrics that can be used against staff in disciplinary or performance review settings.

What is the union's stance on AI use?

“Now people in disciplinary situations are suddenly having read back to them, ‘You only did one [pull request] per week, per whatever, and that’s 25 percent below industry standard,’” says Ben Harnett, a software engineer at The New York Times.

The union has filed an unfair labor practice charge earlier this month, highlighting the need for greater transparency around AI use in the newsroom.

source.title: The AI fight brewing inside The New York Times
source.summary: The New York Times is facing a labor dispute with its employees over the use of artificial intelligence (AI) in newsrooms. The debate on how to integrate AI into journalism has been ongoing within the industry, and now union representatives are negotiating with publishers to establish guidelines for its use. The outcome of these negotiations will determine how AI is used in the newspaper's operations and what impact it may have on jobs and editorial standards.

results.seo_title: DeepSlide: Revolutionizing Presentation Delivery for AI-Driven Slides
results.meta_description: Discover DeepSlide, a human-in-the-loop multi-agent system that optimizes presentation delivery and improves narrative flow, pacing precision, and…
results.body_html:
DeepSlide is a human-in-the-loop multi-agent system that optimizes presentation delivery and improves narrative flow, pacing precision, and slide-script synergy.

Key Features of DeepSlide

  • A controllable logical-chain planner with per-node time budgets for efficient planning
  • A lightweight content-tree retriever for grounding in evidence-based information
  • Markov-style sequential rendering with style inheritance for visually appealing slides
  • Sandboxed execution with minimal repair to ensure renderability and reliability

Benefits of DeepSlide

Narrative Flow Pacing Precision Slide-Script Synergy
Improved narrative flow with clearer attention guidance Precision pacing with minimal errors Enhanced slide-script synergy for engaging audiences

Performance Metrics of DeepSlide

Across 20 domains and diverse audience profiles, DeepSlide consistently achieves larger gains on delivery metrics, improving narrative flow, pacing precision, and slide-script synergy.

source.title: DeepSlide: From Artifacts to Presentation Delivery
source.summary: Presentations are a primary medium for scholarly communication, yet most AI slide generators optimize the artifact (a visually plausible deck) while under-optimizing the delivery process (pacing, narrative, and presentation preparation). We present DeepSlide, a human-in-the-loop multi-agent system that supports preparing the full presentation process, from requirement elicitation and time-budgeted narrative planning, to evidence-grounded slide--script generation, attention augmentation, and rehearsal support. DeepSlide integrates (i) a controllable logical-chain planner with per-node time budgets, (ii) a lightweight content-tree retriever for grounding, (iii) Markov-style sequential rendering with style inheritance, and (iv) sandboxed execution with minimal repair to ensure renderability. We further introduce a dual-scoreboard benchmark that cleanly separates static artifact quality from dynamic delivery excellence. Across 20 domains and diverse audience profiles, DeepSlide matches strong baselines on artifact quality while consistently achieving larger gains on delivery metrics, improving narrative flow, pacing precision, and slide--script synergy with clearer attention guidance.

results.seo_title: NOVA Framework Limits AI Knowledge Discovery
results.meta_description: The NOVA framework models the common generate-verify-accumulate-retrain loop as an adaptive sampling process over a knowledge space, identifying sufficient…
results.body_html:
Can AI systems discover genuinely new knowledge through iterative self improvement, and if so, at what cost?

Failure Modes of Knowledge Discovery

Contamination: false-positive artifacts enter the knowledge base faster than genuine discoveries. Forgetting: accumulated knowledge eventually covers a finite domain. Exploration failure: model fails to explore new knowledge spaces. Acceptance failure: model fails to accept new knowledge.

Cumulative generation cost R_ extcum(D) = Θ(c_ extgenD^ extα)

Human Amplification through Guidance, Generation, and Verification Expert input is most valuable near autonomous exploration barriers.

source.title: NOVA: Fundamental Limits of Knowledge Discovery Through AI
source.summary: Can AI systems discover genuinely new knowledge through iterative self improvement, and if so, at what cost? We introduce the NOVA framework, which models the common ``generate, verify, accumulate, retrain'' loop as an adaptive sampling process over a knowledge space. We identify sufficient conditions under which accumulated genuine knowledge eventually covers a finite domain, and show how their violations produce distinct failure modes: contamination, forgetting, exploration failure, and acceptance failure. We then analyze imperfect verification and identify a contamination trap: as easy-to-find knowledge is exhausted, the model mass assigned to new valid artifacts shrinks, so even small false-positive rates can cause invalid artifacts to enter the knowledge base faster than genuine discoveries. We clarify that Good--Turing estimation is a local batch-diversity diagnostic, not an estimator of the historically undiscovered valid mass that governs long-term discovery. Under a separate tail-equivalence assumption relating the model's effective discovery distribution to a Zipf law with exponent $\alpha>1$, we prove that the cumulative generation cost required to obtain $D$ distinct genuine discoveries satisfies $R_{\mathrm{cum}}(D)=\Theta(c_{\mathrm{gen}}D^\alpha)$, where $c_{\mathrm{gen}}$ is the per-candidate generation cost. This scaling law quantifies asymptotic diminishing returns as the discovery frontier advances. Finally, we formalize human amplification through guidance, generation, and verification, explaining why expert input is most valuable near autonomous exploration barriers.

results.seo_title: AI warfare is already here
results.meta_description: The Convention on Certain Conventional Weapons, an international forum that focuses on lethal autonomous systems, is hosted twice a year at the United Nations…
results.body_html:
AICloseAIPosts from this topic will be added to your daily email digest and your homepage feed. FollowFollowSee All AI

PolicyClosePolicyPosts from this topic will be added to your daily email digest and your homepage feed. FollowFollowSee All Policy

ReportCloseReportPosts from this topic will be added to your daily email digest and your homepage feed. FollowFollowSee All Report

Anthropic’s fight with the Pentagon highlights the risks of autonomous warfare — but obscures just how close it is.

Posts from this author will be added to your daily email digest and your homepage feed.

Posts from this author will be added to your daily email digest and your homepage feed.

The Convention on Certain Conventional Weapons, an international forum that focuses on lethal autonomous systems, is hosted twice a year at the United Nations in Geneva. When Branka Marijan attended in November 2017, she thought the five-day sessions — which dealt largely in hypotheticals, speculating on a world where warfare was fought with killer robots — would be business as usual. After all, this was technology some thought might never be developed, and likely never deployed. That year, she quickly realized, was different. That distant, imagined future was suddenly closer and realer than ever.

On the first day, some attendees watched a short film called Slaughterbots, put together by the Future of Life Institute. The video featured a fictional defense contractor pitching an AI-powered drone that could kill unassisted with precision strikes. “They used to say guns don’t kill people, people do,” its CEO tells the audience. “But people don’t. They get emotional, disobey orders, aim high. Let’s watch the weapons make the decisions.” The mood in the room, Marijan recalls, suddenly turned apprehensive. The most frightening part wasn’t the premise — it was that the Pentagon was already developing a version of this technology.

That meeting was the first one held after the start of Project Maven, a US Department of Defense initiative using AI to analyze drone surveillance footage. And by late 2017, Maven had a major tech company on board: Google. “The systems we were talking about were not futuristic,” said Marijan, who is a senior researcher at Project Ploughshares, a peace-focused independent research institute. “They were existing platforms that had degrees of autonomy in them, or the capability to select and engage targets based on sensor data and sensor input.”

The world had already seen drone warfare — deadly machines directed by humans. Now, it was looking at a future where humans may be removed from the loop entirely. “These were not these Terminator-like figures that we were concerned about, but really what was happening with the enablement of autonomy,” said Marijan.

Nearly a decade later, militaries haven’t yet developed fully autonomous lethal weapons. But these systems sit squarely in the center of a recent high-stakes battle between the US government and AI startup Anthropic. Anthropic is seeking to preserve two “red lines”: bans on domestic mass surveillance and on weapons that can identify, track, and kill targets with zero human involvement. Since the start of the year, it’s emerged as the only military AI contractor to place meaningful limits on what experts call one of the final frontiers of AI warfare.

But amid shifting alliances, lawsuits, and melodrama, it’s easy to lose sight of the larger context — that AI is, and long has been, deeply embedded in the military. Seventy years ago, a summer meeting between scientists in New Hampshire made the Department of Defense sit up and take notice of AI’s potential for war. Since then, its influence has grown exponentially every decade. In recent years in particular, AI has enabled more and faster killings than ever before.

Even Anthropic seems to think its red lines won’t hold for long. After all, history has proven otherwise.

The US military has backed AI development for decades, and in turn, AI has transformed warfare. In the 2000s, the technology became capable of parsing unprecedented amounts of data collected worldwide, creating a surveillance revolution. And the late 2010s saw the development of advanced facial recognition and other sophisticated machine vision systems.

The fight between Anthropic and the Pentagon has drawn attention to the growing power of these systems. It began in January of 2026, when Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth demanded a renegotiation of the DOD’s existing AI contracts. The terms did away with any gray areas or previously agreed-upon terms, allowing the Pentagon to use the companies’ technology within the vague and expansive limits of “any lawful use.” Anthropic — the only AI company approved to deploy its tech on the Pentagon’s classified networks at that point — objected.

A contractor like Anthropic setting limits on specific uses of its tech is unusual. “It’s not government-created technology in the way that the Manhattan Project was,” nor a conventional military supplier like Northrop Grumman, said Andrew Reddie, an associate research professor of public policy at the University of California, Berkeley. “This is one of the pain points that’s made clear when you’ve got this startup ecosystem engaging with the Pentagon directly.” Even within Silicon Valley, he said, there’s “a lot of disagreement” over when limits should be set.

It’s also unclear who will win. As a negotiation tactic, the DOD designated Anthropic a military supply chain risk in March, and President Donald Trump declared he was banning all government agencies from using its Claude system. The relationship has apparently warmed somewhat since then, with the release of Anthropic’s cybersecurity-focused model Mythos, but a court battle is still playing out. Anthropic declined to provide a comment for this story.

Either way, the debate has brought “fully autonomous weapons” into the public lexicon in a new way. But AI’s creeping influence in military operations hasn’t slowed down in decades.

“We’ve kind of crossed the rubicon while we pretend that we haven’t,” Reddie said.

At the center of the debates is DOD Directive 3000.09, one of the only policies governing the use of lethal autonomous weapons. Originally written in 2012, it defines such a system as one that, “once activated, can select and engage targets without further intervention by an operator.” And it decrees that both fully autonomous and semi-autonomous weapons be designed to allow humans to “exercise appropriate levels” of judgment over the use of force.

The directive set up the “first policy on the use of autonomy in warfare,” said Hamza Chaudhry, who leads AI and national security at the Future of Life Institute.

“Even without full autonomy, AI compresses kill chains to mere seconds.”

Depending on how you interpret the definition, however, certain missile defense programs may have crossed that line decades ago. Take the Phalanx CIWS, for instance. It’s an automated weapon system resembling a very large gun, built to defend naval vessels from incoming missile attacks. That type of system wouldn’t work if there were a human in the loop, since it has to respond in milliseconds.

The difference, some experts say, is that these systems operate solely in a defense-only, fixed environment. They’re engaging, this interpretation goes, but not deciding — just reacting to an incoming threat. “The ‘and’ is doing a lot of work inside of that statute — we have systems that can decide and systems that can engage but you can’t have a system that does both,” Reddie said.

“Even without full autonomy, AI compresses kill chains to mere seconds so that humans are not actually making the assessments that international humanitarian law requires to prevent civilian harm,” said Maddy Batt, legal fellow at Tech Justice Law. “When humans’ failure to do that results in civilian death, that is a war crime.”

The definition between offensive and defensive, too, is fuzzy. “One of my favorite exercises with my students is putting up a military technology and then asking, ‘Is this defensive or offensive?’” Reddie said, pointing to the example of a nuclear weapon in a silo, which some would argue is defense because it’s being used to deter, and some would argue is offense because it’s designed to hit foreign targets. “Just because its primary function … is defensive in nature doesn’t mean the technology itself is defensive in nature.”

Sorin Adam Matei, a professor at Purdue University, was blunter: “You cannot fight a war only in defense.”

In 2023, the government’s Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Office (CDAO) — which is the central hub of all the DOD’s AI operations — issued an update to DOD Directive 3000.09. But it didn’t resolve the document’s core ambiguities. In 2024, the Biden administration published a memorandum on AI and national security, setting up rules for how AI can be used in certain national security scenarios — and for now, even under Trump, that policy is in force. But the Pentagon has seen significant upheaval. The CDAO is currently undergoing a significant restructuring that makes it more isolated from the rest of the DOD, and the office now reports to Emil Michael, who is both the DOD’s undersecretary of research and engineering and the department’s CTO.

source.title: AI warfare is already here
source.summary:
Here is a summary of the news item in 2-4 sentences:

The Convention on Certain Conventional Weapons (CCW) is an international forum that addresses lethal autonomous systems. The CCW meets twice a year at the United Nations in Geneva, where it discusses hypothetical scenarios involving killer robots and other advanced technologies. In 2017, Branka Marijan attended one of these sessions, indicating that discussions about AI warfare have been ongoing for several years.

results.seo_title: Understanding Metacognitive AI: A Key to More Accurate and Secure AI…
results.meta_description: Discover the benefits of metacognitive AI and how it can improve the accuracy, security, and efficiency of AI systems.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: Metacognitive AI is a design principle that involves systems monitoring their own states and allocating resources based on problem instance difficulty or cost of mistakes.

Challenges in Embedding Metacognition into AI DesignSpecific challenges identified in embedding metacognitive strategies into AI designOpen theoretical and implementation problems highlighted in the paperTangible Example: Improved Learning Efficiency and Security in Federated LearningKey MetricsDescriptionImproved learning efficiencyMetacognitive AI enables more efficient learning with improved accuracy and securityEnhanced effectivenessMetacognitive AI improves the overall effectiveness of AI systemsIncreased securityMetacognitive AI enhances the security of AI systems by monitoring their own statesNovel Software Framework for Metacognition-Enabled AI ApplicationsDeveloped specifically to allow the community to design, deploy, and experiment with metacognition-enabled AI applications

source.title: Position: Artificial Intelligence Needs Meta Intelligence -- the Case for Metacognitive AI
source.summary: This position paper argues for metacognition as a general design principle for creating more accurate, secure, and efficient AI. The metacognitive solution involves systems monitoring their own states and judiciously allocating resources depending on each problem instance's difficulty or cost of mistakes. Drawing inspiration both from past work on resource-rational AI and from well-documented metacognitive strategies in psychology and cognitive science, we identify specific challenges in embedding these strategies into AI design and highlight open theoretical and implementation problems. We showcase these principles through a tangible example of improved learning efficiency, effectiveness, and security in a Federated Learning (FL) case study. We show how these principles can be translated into practice with a novel software framework developed specifically to allow the community to design, deploy, and experiment with metacognition-enabled AI applications.

results.seo_title: Why People Prefer AI-Generated Music Over Real Artists' Work
results.meta_description: A growing trend in the Suno subreddit shows people favoring AI-generated music over traditional artists' work, but what's behind this preference?
results.body_html:
People are increasingly turning to AI-generated music, but why?

The music I've generated is without a doubt better matched to my taste than most music made by real artists/bands. I can get the far out genres — country/rap and electronic dance/rap — that I just can't find elsewhere.

AI-generated music is tailored to individual tastes, offering a unique listening experience. No more generic playlists or music recommendations.

Benefits of AI-Generated Music FeatureDescription ConvenienceNo need to search for music or create playlists. DiscoveryDiscover new genres and artists through AI-generated music.

Limitations and Concerns While AI-generated music offers unique benefits, it also raises concerns about the role of human creativity in music production.

source.title: Nobody wants to tell me why they only listen to their own Suno slop
source.summary: There is a concerning trend among users of the Suno subreddit, where individuals are spending most of their time listening to AI-generated songs and have largely abandoned traditional music streaming platforms. Some users even proudly claim that they only consume AI-created content, with no interest in human-made music. This behavior has raised concerns among others on the platform.

The breach was reported by the state government, which listed the compromised personal data. Details about the cause and timing of the breach are not provided in the excerpt.

results.seo_title: Spotify now lets you stream narrated magazine articles, too
results.meta_description: The streaming service said that, starting today, there will be over 650 long-form magazine articles (available in English only) from publications like Rolling…
results.body_html: The streaming service said that, starting today, there will be over 650 long-form magazine articles (available in English only) from publications like Rolling Stone, The Atlantic, Vogue, Variety, Billboard, Vibe, GQ, Wired, Vanity Fair, and Pitchfork.

source.title: Spotify now lets you stream narrated magazine articles, too
source.summary: Spotify has expanded its content offerings to include narrated magazine articles, with over 650 titles available starting today. These articles come from prominent publications such as Rolling Stone, The Atlantic, and Vogue, and are currently only in English. The addition is part of Spotify's efforts to provide users with a wider range of engaging audio content.

results.seo_title: Universal Music Group and TikTok Renew Agreement to Combat…
results.meta_description: UMG and TikTok renew agreement to combat unauthorized AI music, following years of push for stricter content moderation policies.
results.body_html:
UMG and TikTok have renewed their agreement to combat unauthorized AI music, following years of push for stricter content moderation policies.

TikTok has committed to implementing more robust content moderation measures to prevent the use of UMG's music in AI-generated content without permission. UMG will continue to work closely with TikTok to ensure that its music is protected from unauthorized use in AI applications.

Policy Requirement Implementation Date

Stricter content moderation policies for AI companies Implemented by UMG and TikTok in 2026

The renewed agreement is expected to have a significant impact on the music industry, as it sets a new standard for content moderation in AI applications. UMG and TikTok's partnership demonstrates the growing importance of protecting intellectual property in the digital age.

source.title: Universal Music Group and TikTok renew agreement to combat unauthorized AI music
source.summary: The Universal Music Group (UMG) and TikTok have renewed their agreement to combat the use of unauthorized AI-generated music on the platform. This move aims to protect creators' rights and prevent the misuse of artificial intelligence in music production. UMG has been advocating for stricter content moderation policies among platforms, streaming services, and AI companies for years.

results.seo_title: Iranian Hackers Blamed for Los Angeles Transit System Breach
results.meta_description: A recent breach of the Los Angeles transit system has been attributed to Iranian hackers, with weeks-long recovery efforts underway.
results.body_html:
Iranian hackers have been blamed for the breach of the Los Angeles transit system, which took weeks to recover.

Key Facts About the BreachThe breach was attributed to Iranian hackers by an Israeli cybersecurity firm.The fake hacktivist persona Ababil of Minab claimed a series of data breaches after the start of the war in Iran.The recovery efforts took weeks, highlighting the complexity and potential risks of such attacks.Consequences for the IndustryImpact on the Transit SystemIndustry-wide ImplicationsData breaches can compromise sensitive information, leading to financial losses and reputational damage.The incident highlights the need for robust cybersecurity measures in critical infrastructure sectors.Security Measures in PlaceImplementing advanced threat detection systems and conducting

regular security audits can help prevent similar breaches in the future.

source.title: Iranian hackers blamed for breach of Los Angeles transit system that took weeks to recover
source.summary: A cyber attack on the Los Angeles transit system took weeks to recover from, and an Israeli cybersecurity firm has blamed it on Iranian hackers using a fake hacktivist persona called Ababil of Minab. The attack is linked to the start of the war in Iran. Details about the breach are not provided in the excerpt.

results.seo_title: Ghost Hackers: The Cybersecurity Mystery That Nobody Has Solved
results.meta_description: A shadowy group stole the NSA's most powerful hacking tools, leaving companies to rethink digital risk.
results.body_html:
A shadowy group that stole and dumped the NSA’s most powerful hacking tools still has implications for how companies think about digital risk today.

Key Implications for Digital Risk Management

The stolen tools have left a significant gap in the cybersecurity landscape, making it challenging for companies to assess and mitigate risks. Companies must now consider alternative solutions that can fill the gap left by the stolen tools. The incident highlights the need for robust cybersecurity measures, including regular software updates and patching.

The Impact on Industry and Job Market

Impact on IndustryJob Market Implications

The incident has raised concerns about the security of sensitive information, leading to increased demand for cybersecurity professionals. The job market is expected to see an increase in demand for experts with skills in threat intelligence and incident response.

What Can Be Learned from the Incident? The stolen tools have shown that even the most powerful hacking tools can be compromised, highlighting the need for constant vigilance and improvement in cybersecurity measures.

source.title: Ghost hackers: the cybersecurity mystery that nobody has solved
source.summary: A mysterious group of "ghost hackers" stole and leaked the National Security Agency's (NSA) most powerful hacking tools, leaving behind significant cybersecurity implications. The incident highlights the ongoing threat of cyber attacks on sensitive information and the need for robust digital security measures. Despite efforts to track down those responsible, the identity and motivations of the ghost hackers remain unknown.

source.title: Dutch government blocks US company from acquisition, citing ‘risk to public interest’
source.summary: The Dutch government has blocked a proposed acquisition by a US company, citing concerns over potential risks to the public interest. The decision affects a cloud company that hosts the Netherlands' digital ID service. This move is part of Europe's efforts to reduce its reliance on US technology.

results.seo_title: SpaceX's Starlink Wins American Airlines Contract
results.meta_description: American Airlines plans to install Starlink on over 500 Airbus aircraft, a win for SpaceX's IPO.
results.body_html:
SpaceX's Starlink has won another major contract, this time with American Airlines. The airline plans to install Starlink on over 500 Airbus aircraft, a significant win for SpaceX ahead of its IPO.

Key Details

  • Number of aircraft: Over 500 Airbus planes
  • Carrier win: Latest contract for SpaceX's IPO-bound company
  • Installation plan: To be completed in phases

Impact on Industry

The installation of Starlink on American Airlines' fleet is expected to have a significant impact on the airline's operations and customer experience.

BenefitDescription
Improved in-flight connectivityStarlink will provide faster and more reliable internet access for passengers
Enhanced safety featuresThe system will enable pilots to stay connected with air traffic control during flights

Technical Specifications

Starlink will be installed on Airbus A320 and A321 aircraft, with the installation process expected to take several months.

source.title: SpaceX’s Starlink nabs American Airlines contract, another win for its IPO
source.summary: SpaceX's Starlink satellite internet service has secured a contract with American Airlines to be installed on over 500 of its Airbus aircraft. This is another significant win for SpaceX ahead of its upcoming initial public offering (IPO). The deal marks an expansion of Starlink's reach into the aviation industry.
